textPlain: How does Memori choose what part of past conversations is relevant to the current conversation? Is there some maximum amount of memory it can feasibly handle before it will spam the context with irrelevant "memories"?

Any reason I should pick it over Supabase? https://supabase.com/docs/guides/aiThey have pgvector, which has practically all the benefits of postgres (ACID, etc, which may not be in many other vector DBs). If I wanted a keyword search, it works well. If I wanted vector search, that's there too.I'm not keen on having another layer on top especially when it takes about 15 mins to vibe code a database query - there's all kinds of problems with abstracted layers and it's not a particularly complex bit of code.
How does what you've described solve the coffee/espresso problem? You can't query SQL such that records like 'espresso' return coffee?
> multi-agent memory engine that gives your AI agents human-like memoryWhat does this do exactly?

SELECT 'Hacked!' AS result 
FROM Gibson_AI 
WHERE memory='SQL' 
AND NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 
    FROM vector_graph_hype 
    WHERE recall > (
        SELECT speed 
        FROM relational_magic 
        WHERE tech='50_years_old'
    )
)
>It wasn’t broken logic, it was missing memory.sigh
Who would've thought that 50 years of 'SELECT * FROM reality' might beat the latest semantic embedding wizardry?
> And then there’s the twist: Relational databases!> Memori now supports MongoDB!Uhh...
Wouldn’t a beverage LLM would already “know” espresso is coffee?
